coredata.dc:description: Phase change heat storage is the backbone of energy storage technology, but its storage time is affected by the low thermal conductivity of phase change materials. Therefore, the melting performance of a triplex-tube latent heat thermal energy storage unit (T-LHTESU) in a phase change heat storage system is studied in this paper, and the rotation mechanism is applied to the unit. Firstly, a numerical model of the T-LHTESU considering the rotation mechanism is constructed, and the validity of the rotation unit is verified by comparison with experimental data. In this unit, N-eicosane is used as a phase change material for heat exchange. The effects of different rotational speeds on the liquid phase distribution, temperature distribution, flow velocity distribution, total energy storage, and energy storage efficiency of the T-LHTESU are studied. The results show that the melting time of this unit at 0.1 and 1 rpm is 46.98 and 69.35% lower than that of the stationary model, respectively. The total amount of stored heat is decreased by 0.67 and 2.17%, and the heat storage efficiency is increased by 87.34% and 219.19%, respectively. This indicates that the addition of the rotation mechanism greatly increases the heat storage efficiency of the T-LHTESU and reduces its total melting time, while the reduction of the total energy stored in the melting cycle is small. Then it is proved that rotation improves the single heat transfer mechanism of the stationary model and eliminates the thermal deposition caused by natural convection by studying the internal temperature/velocity response of the stationary model and the speed of 0.1 rpm. The related geometric structure of the model is optimized by response surface optimization design based on 0.1 rpm rotation speed. The influence of each variable on the target response is obtained, and compared with the original model, the melting time of the optimized model is reduced by 12.24%. Finally, based on the geometric optimization design, the influence of element physical factors (temperature and material of fin/tube wall) on the related melting properties is studied. This study is helpful to promote the effective use of rotation mechanism in phase change heat storage systems and has a certain guiding role in the structural design.

head.ce:title: Investigation and optimization on melting performance of a triplex-tube heat storage tank by rotational mechanism

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Phase change heat storage is the backbone of energy storage technology, but its storage time is affected by the low thermal conductivity of phase change materials. Therefore, the melting performance of a triplex-tube latent heat thermal energy storage unit (T-LHTESU) in a phase change heat storage system is studied in this paper, and the rotation mechanism is applied to the unit. Firstly, a numerical model of the T-LHTESU considering the rotation mechanism is constructed, and the validity of the rotation unit is verified by comparison with experimental data. In this unit, N-eicosane is used as a phase change material for heat exchange. The effects of different rotational speeds on the liquid phase distribution, temperature distribution, flow velocity distribution, total energy storage, and energy storage efficiency of the T-LHTESU are studied. The results show that the melting time of this unit at 0.1 and 1 rpm is 46.98 and 69.35% lower than that of the stationary model, respectively. The total amount of stored heat is decreased by 0.67 and 2.17%, and the heat storage efficiency is increased by 87.34% and 219.19%, respectively. This indicates that the addition of the rotation mechanism greatly increases the heat storage efficiency of the T-LHTESU and reduces its total melting time, while the reduction of the total energy stored in the melting cycle is small. Then it is proved that rotation improves the single heat transfer mechanism of the stationary model and eliminates the thermal deposition caused by natural convection by studying the internal temperature/velocity response of the stationary model and the speed of 0.1 rpm. The related geometric structure of the model is optimized by response surface optimization design based on 0.1 rpm rotation speed. The influence of each variable on the target response is obtained, and compared with the original model, the melting time of the optimized model is reduced by 12.24%. Finally, based on the geometric optimization design, the influence of element physical factors (temperature and material of fin/tube wall) on the related melting properties is studied. This study is helpful to promote the effective use of rotation mechanism in phase change heat storage systems and has a certain guiding role in the structural design.

ce:list-item.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: With the continuous development of the world economy and industrialization, global energy consumption is increasing significantly. Fossil energy accounts for the highest proportion of energy utilization, but the use of fossil energy brings severe environmental pollution [1]. The effective use of renewable energy plays an important role in shifting the current unreasonable energy structure, providing an alternate solution to the urgent task of environmental cleaning and environmental remedy [2]. However, the inherent unpredictability and intermittency of renewable energy increase the difficulty of practical extensive use. Energy storage technology is the core support for the development of clean energy and renewable energy [3–5].

Thermal energy storage (TES) is one of the most promising large-scale energy storage technologies [6]. Compared with electrochemical energy storage [7], electrical energy storage [8] and other related technologies, thermal energy storage technology has certain advantages in terms of installed scale, technical cost, service life, and energy storage density [9]. In addition, thermal energy storage technology has high energy density, efficient conversion, and reasonable application cost of large capacity large-scale energy storage. Thus, it is the backbone of future energy storage [10,11]. Thermal energy storage technology can be mainly divided into sensible heat storage [12], latent heat storage [13], and thermochemical energy storage [14], and the first two are widely used. Based on phase change materials (PCMs) as the medium, latent heat energy storage systems (LHTES) can store energy by absorbing/releasing the heat of phase change materials. It has the advantages of large heat storage, strong stability, low cost, and small temperature fluctuation, and has a wide application prospect [15]. However, the low thermal conductivity of PCM leads to poor thermal response and efficiency [16]. In this paper, the literature analysis software VOSviewer is used to screen the keywords of LHTES technology-related publications since 2017, and the keywords that appeared more than 100 times are screened, as shown in Fig. 1 . It is found that systematic studies of LHTES technology appear most frequently, followed by those on thermal conductivity. This shows that the low thermal conductivity of PCM has always been the focus of LHTES technology research.

There are two main methods to enhance the thermal properties of LHTES technology: improving the heating area of PCM (adding metal fins [17] or metal foam materials [18]) or improving the thermal performance of PCM (packaging PCM [19] or adding nanoparticles [20]). Among these, the application of embedded metal fins in LHTES technology has been largely developed by researchers in the past decades due to its good performance and low cost, including fractal fins, triangular fins, circular/annular fins, tree-shaped fins, and rectangular fins, etc. [21]. However, the use of metal fins will lead to a decrease in the volume of PCM in LHTES. How to improve the heat transfer efficiency while ensuring the volume of PCM is worth further study [22]. Huang et al. [22] designed a new triplex-tube latent heat storage system and carried out a numerical study. The response surface method was applied to optimize the structure and keep the internal PCM volume unaffected. The results showed that the optimized structure reduces the melting time of the system by 23.87%. Khatibi et al. [23] added different metal oxide nanoparticles into the triplex-tube LHTES system and introduced the Taguchi design method to determine the effects of different factors on its energy storage performance. The results showed that the CuO with a volume fraction of 0.04 has the best effect, and the solidification time is shortened by 15.7%. Compared with the pipe diameter and heat transfer fluid temperature, the element type of internal and external heating had the most significant influence. Xiao et al. [24] established a new LSTM-BP neural network for a new foam metal LHTS system. The influence of HTF inlet speed, temperature, and time was studied. The results showed that the predicted results of machine learning are in good agreement with the numerical results, and the increase of HTF speed/ temperature has a significant effect. Zhang et al. [25] conducted an experimental study on the heat transfer enhancement process of metal foam/paraffin composite phase change materials. The model of the PCM melting process of composite materials was fitted by experimental data. Li et al. [26] studied the melting process of LHTES system with twisted fins of different positions and structures through numerical simulation. Exergy analysis and exergy efficiency studies were carried out in addition to the related melting properties. The results showed that the two horizontal fins and the three vertical fins have the best effect, which can improve the melting performance by 30.5 and 37.4%, and have the best exergy efficiency. For a triplex-tube heat storage device, Hosseinzadeh et al. [27,28] used a mixture of MoS2-Fe3O4 nanoparticles and innovative fins to improve its solidification rate. The Taguchi method and response surface method were used for optimization, and the numerical model was proved at the same time. The results showed that the optimized model reduces the longest solidification time, and the radiation parameters have the greatest influence on the solidification time. In a TES device, Mehryan et al. [29] studied the melting behavior of composite phase change materials. Taguchi method was used to optimize the porosity, shape, and volume fraction of nanoparticles of a copper foam. The optimal structure can reduce the melting properties by 21.96%. Ge et al. [30] applied topology optimization in a shell-and-tube LHTES, generated topology optimized fin structure for its melting process, and compared it with the foam metal structure. The results showed that the topological fin structure can obtain the highest energy storage efficiency and improve the melting efficiency by 88%. Li et al. [31] studied the influence of topology optimized fins, foam metal and composite phase change materials on the melting performance of tube-and-shell energy storage devices. Experiments were designed to verify the numerical models of the topology optimized fins and metal foam. The results showed that the melting time of the system is reduced by 86% and 88%, respectively. Finally, it is suggested that the price between topological fin and PCM can be controlled below 8 to obtain better strengthening effect and economic benefits. Tian et al. [32] used the bionic topology optimization method to improve the heat storage/release of latent heat storage technology and considered the natural convection in the phase transformation process. The solidification/melting time of the system is reduced by 80% and 93%, respectively, and the fractal of the optimized fin is like that of the blade. The above studies have all adopted high-performance fins to enhance the heat transfer process of LHTES, including different fin structures. Taguchi method, response surface method, and topology optimization method were used to optimize the fin design, and good results were achieved.

In recent studies on heat transfer enhancement of LHTES, some scholars have innovatively applied rotation mechanism to LHTES technology. Kurnia and Sasmito [33] established a computational fluid dynamics (CFD) model of a latent heat storage system and verified it by experiments, and applied rotation to its storage/heat release process The heat transfer performance of the system increased by 25% and 41%, respectively, and the higher the rotational speed is, the higher the heat transfer rate is. Soltani et al. [34,35] studied the phase transition performance of the LHTES system by an enthalpy-porosity method and applied the rotation mechanism to it. The heat transfer ratio of storage and release increased by 2.45 times and 3.87 times, respectively, with the increase in rotational speed. Then, the response surface optimization method was applied to the rotating LHTES system, with fin, rotational speed, and nanoparticle content as variables. The results showed that the rotational speed had the greatest influence on the heat transfer efficiency of the system. The optimal model reduces the melting/solidification time by 74 and 70%, and the storage/release PCM capacity by 2.5% and 4.1%, respectively. Fathi and Mussa [36] carried out experimental studies using 3, 6, and 9 rpm rotational speeds in shell-and-tube LHTES units. The results showed that the enhancement rate of liquid fraction reached 8.12% during the operating time compared with the static structure. Khosroshahi and Hossainpour [37] established a two-dimensional numerical model for a rotating double-tube LHTES, in which they compared the schemes of constant rotation speed and distributed rotation. The constant rotation speed reduced the melting time by 3.45%, while the distributed rotation scheme reduced the melting time by 15.5%. Modi et al. [38] adopted a new technology of intermittent rotation for the heat transfer process of an eccentric LHTES system. The results showed that the optimal bottom eccentricity is between 0.60 and 0.75, which can increase the melting efficiency by 64–74%. HTF temperature and shell radius ratio have a minor effect on the optimal eccentricity of the system. The influence of different rotation modes (static, rotation, and reversal) on the melting properties of shell-and-tube LHTES for the problem of uneven melting was studied by Yang et al. [39]. The rotation strengthening measures have the highest improvement in melting performance, which increased the heat storage rate by 18.54% and shortened the melting time by 19.35%. When the temperature of inlet HTF is raised by 10 K, the heat storage rate is increased by 5% and the melting time is shortened by 65%. The re-solidification of liquid PCM was also observed in the experiment. Yu et al. [40] studied the influence of constant rotation and 180° single rotation on the heat storage process of a LHTES system. The results showed that when the rotational speed is 0.5 rpm, the total melting time of the system can be reduced by 533.7% with a single rotation, while the increase of the rotational speed at 1 rpm is not significant. The above studies show that the application of the rotation mechanism in LHTES has achieved good results and has a great role in promoting the improvement of its storage/release heat efficiency. However, the reason why rotation improves the heat transfer mode in the melting process is still worth exploring.

Therefore, based on the previous research results, this paper applies the rotation mechanism to a triplex-tube latent heat thermal energy storage unit, and the improvement of its melting performance has been studied in detail. A two-dimensional numerical model of this T-LHTESU with a rotation mechanism is established, and the usability of the model is validated. The effects of different rotational speeds on the melting performance (liquid fraction, heat absorption and energy storage efficiency) of the energy storage device are studied. Through the study of the dynamic response of internal temperature and velocity, the improvement of the melting process by rotation is further discussed. Then, the response surface method is used to optimize the geometric elements of the multi-parameter model. Finally, the influence of element physical factors (tube wall temperature and finned tube wall material) on the melting performance is studied. In this study, the rotational mechanism is applied for the first time in the melting process of a triplex-tube latent heat thermal energy storage unit, and has a certain research value for its structural optimization design.

A 3-D structure diagram of a horizontal triplex-tube latent heat thermal energy storage unit (T-LHTESU) studied is shown in Fig. 2 (a), which is composed of three concentric tubes with a length of 200 mm. The inner/outer tubes are used for the inflow of heat transfer fluid (HTF), and the intermediate tube is used to fill phase change material (PCM). The advantage lies in its double-sided heating characteristics and relatively short phase change time (melting/solidification) compared with conventional double-tube LHTES units [41]. Based on the model, the applied torque is added to ensure that the T-LHTESU rotates at a constant speed during melting. Four rectangular fins are crisscross connected to the outside of the inner tube and the inside of the outer tube to increase the heat transfer performance of the unit to achieve a smaller melting time, and the fins are consistent with the shell material.

The two-dimensional cross-section of the T-LHTESU is shown in Fig. 2(b). The model is simplified because of the high velocity of HTF and the small temperature difference in the element's radial direction. The outer diameter of the inner tube (R 1) is 10 mm, the inner diameter of the intermediate tube (R 2) is 20 mm, and the outer diameter (R 3) is 35 mm. The staggered rectangular fin length (L) is 7 mm, width (w) is 0.5 mm. For the following transient analysis of temperature and flow velocity inside the unit, the quartering point of the intermediate tube is selected in the directions of 22.5°, 45°, and 67.5°, as shown in Points 1–9 in Fig. 2(b). As shown in Fig. 2(c) is the numerical model of this unit. The inner and outer wall surfaces are simplified as constant temperature walls, shown as Wall-1 and Wall-2, respectively. The positive rotation direction is the counterclockwise direction shown in ω. The rectangular fins can be deflected around the root of the fins, the positive direction θ of the inner and outer tube fins is shown in Fig. 2(c), and the deflection occurs at the same time. Considering the availability of materials, N-eicosane is used as the PCM, and copper is set as the material of tube wall/fins. The physical properties of related materials are shown in Table 1 .

In addition, the following assumptions are introduced [35]:
(1)
The liquid PCM is considered to be incompressible laminar flow.
(2)
The Boussinesq hypothesis is used to consider the natural convection of melting processes caused by density changes.
(3)
The thermophysical properties of PCM are independent of temperature.
(4)
No heat transfer between the tube housing and the environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment and validation of numerical model
Numerical process construction and usability analysis
ce:section.ce:para: This research work is based on the finite volume method in the CFD software ANSYS FLUENT 18.2 to develop the numerical model. The SIMPLE algorithm is used for pressure-velocity coupling, discretizing the energy and momentum equations using the QUICK format, and the pressure equations are handled with the PRESTO scheme. To improve the stability and convergence of the calculation, the underrelaxation factors of energy, liquid fraction, momentum, and pressure are set to 0.8, 0.9, 0.7, and 0.3, respectively. The absolute convergence principles of the continuum equation, momentum equation, and energy equation are 10−4, 10−4, and 10−8, respectively, to obtain convergent transient solutions. To apply the rotation mechanism to the model, a rotating reference coordinate system is applied to all computing domains.

ANSYS ICEM software is used to divide the computational domain of the model into grids, and unstructured grids are used to establish the grids. To capture the gradient in the melting process better, grid encryption is carried out on the four fins and their adjacent areas, as shown in Fig. 3 .

To make the numerical process more accurate, the grid independence and time step independence of the model are analyzed. As shown in Fig. 4(a), the effect of the different number of computational nodes on the melting process, but rotation is not increased at this time. Combined with Fig. 4(c), for the comparison of the complete melting time, it can be found that when the number of computational nodes is 10,453, the total time gap is large, while the difference is almost negligible when the number of computational nodes is greater than 20,135. To ensure grid independence, the number of computational nodes in this work is kept at about 4 × 104. The verification of time step independence is shown in Fig. 4(b), and the influence of different time steps on the complete melting time is shown in Fig. 4(c). In contrast to the large error when the time step is 0.1 s, the time step of 0.05, 0.02 and 0.01 s can satisfy the independence of the time step. Therefore, considering the accuracy of the calculation results, the time step used in this work is 0.02 s.

In this part, the accuracy of the static T-LHTESU melting process is verified by the experimental data of Al-Abidi et al. [48]. Then, the reliability of the rotary T-LHTESU numerical model is verified through the research data of Soltani et al. [34]. In the verification process, the working conditions of the numerical model are completely consistent with the initial conditions and boundary conditions in the literature. First, the average PCM temperature of the static T-LHTESU melting process is compared, as shown in Fig. 5(a). The relative error between the simulated data and the experimental data is lower than 3.17%, which is reasonable. Then, the changes in the liquid phase fraction and average temperature of PCM at 0.1 rpm are compared, as shown in Fig. 5(b) and (c). It can be seen that the maximum relative error of the liquid phase evolution and the maximum difference of the average temperature of the PCM during the complete melting process are less than 4.2% and 0.47 K, respectively. Therefore, based on the comparative analysis results of model validation, the numerical method used in this study is reliable.

In the melting study of T-LHTESU, the quantitative study of liquid phase evolution, melting time, heat absorption and heat absorption rate is very significant. The effect of rotation speed on the melting properties of T-LHTESU is first studied. A comparison of multiple parameters is carried out by adding different rotation speeds. The liquid phase distribution, temperature distribution, and flow velocity distribution at different times are investigated, and the liquid phase evolution law, melting time, sensible/latent heat absorption and heat absorption rate with the change of rotational speed are quantitatively studied. Then the temperature and flow velocity are monitored through the internal points of T-LHTESU, and the specific influence of rotation on the internal mechanism of the T-LHTESU melting process is studied. By response surface method, multi-parameter optimization of T-LHTESU is carried out with melting time as the optimization objective, and the influence of each parameter on the optimization objective is investigated. Finally, the effect of different physical conditions on the melting properties of T-LHTESU is studied. The liquid phase evolution, melting time, sensible/latent heat absorption and thermal energy absorption rate are also quantitatively studied.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of rotation mechanism on melting process
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the no-rotation model, different rotation speeds (0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1 rpm) are added to study the effect of rotation on the melting behavior of T-LHTESU. Fig. 6 shows the cloud diagrams of T-LHTESU liquid phase distribution and temperature distribution at different times under different rotational speeds. The heat transfer process of the no-rotation model is mainly dominated by heat conduction caused by temperature differences and natural convection inside PCM. The melting degree of the upper part and the lower part of the model is inconsistent at 180 s, because of the action of thermal buoyancy caused by natural convection. The thermal buoyancy at this time leads to deposition of the lower part of the temperature distribution when t = 180 s, and the inhomogeneity of melting also leads to a reduction in the melting rate at the end of the melting.

Then the model is studied by adding different rotational speeds. Taking the rotation speed of 0.05 rpm as an example, the addition of rotation changes the single heat conduction and natural convection under the static condition. It not only brings forced convection but also increases the solid PCM in contact with high-temperature fins with the rotation of fins, which greatly accelerates the melting rate of the refraction zone in the stationary model. When t = 60 s, a large number of liquid PCM are left in the area swept by the fin, and the liquid PCM area in the front part of the fin rotation decreases compared with the static state. By comparing with the temperature cloud image, it is also found that the low-temperature PCM is mainly concentrated in the front part of the fin rotation. When t = 180 s, the unmelted zone is concentrated in the front part of the fin rotation, where the low-temperature zone left by rotation is concentrated. This indicates that the addition of rotation accelerates the heat conduction of fins to PCM, and the forced convection also promotes the melting of PCM. As the rotation speed increases, the PCM range swept by the fin increases in unit time, and the influence of forced convection also increases. Therefore, when t = 120 s, the higher the rotation speed, the less unmelted PCM in T-LHTESU, and the smaller the area of the internal low-temperature zone. By comparison of the rotation speeds of 0.5 rpm and 1 rpm, it is found that only a small part of the unmelted region in front of the fin rotation is left, and the overall temperature distribution and liquid phase distribution are basically similar. When t = 180 s, the remaining solid PCM of 0.1 rpm is only a small area in the upper left. All models with rotational speeds higher than 0.2 rpm have completed the melting process, and only the internal temperature gradient is different.

As shown in Fig. 7 , it is the internal flow velocity distribution of T-LHTESU at different rotational speeds. The initial internal velocity of the no-rotation model is mainly concentrated around the fins and tube wall, which is caused by the initial heat conduction and natural convection. As the melting progresses, it gradually develops to the refraction zone of the model, and the overall velocity is relatively fixed. When t = 240 s, the maximum flow velocity increases by 35.25% compared with t = 120 s. This is due to the diminishing influence of natural convection as melting progresses, and heat transfer is mainly caused by heat conduction between solid and liquid PCM, which makes the melting rate at the later stage smaller and more stable. When rotation is added, such as 0.5 rpm at t = 60 s, an irregular square streamline is formed in the swept area behind fin rotation. The maximum internal flow velocity increased by 136.36% compared with the no-rotation model. However, at the end of melting (t = 240 s), the maximum internal velocity is lower than for the no-rotation model. This is due to the low applied speed, and the rotation needs to overcome the influence of the viscous force inside the PCM. However, the low flow velocity changes the internal heat transfer mechanism and accelerates the heat transfer performance in the middle melting stage. This phenomenon can also be found for the rotation speeds of 0.1 rpm and 0.2 rpm. When t = 240 s, the maximum velocity inside these two rotation models is lower than for the no-rotation state. At this time, the internal PCMs of 0.1 and 0.2 rpm are completely melted liquid, and the influence of the viscous force led to the decrease in internal flow velocity. The rotational speeds of 0.5 and 1 rpm greatly improve the internal flow velocity of the model at different times, which greatly enhances the internal heat transfer performance of T-LHTESU. It is worth noting that when t = 240 s, the highest internal flow velocity of 1 rpm is about twice that of 0.5 rpm, which is also consistent with the magnitude of applied speed.

Fig. 8 shows the specific effects of rotation on the melting properties of T-LHTESU, including (a) liquid fraction of PCM; (b) melting rate; (c) PCM average temperature; (d) melting time and percentage of time saved; (e) sensible heat absorption; (f) total heat absorption; (g) comparison of absorbed heat; (h) heat absorption rate. It can be seen that rotation greatly accelerates the evolution of liquid fraction from Fig. 8(a), while the melting rate in Fig. 8(b) shows that the addition of low rotational speed improves the phenomenon of low melting rate in the static state for a long time and the melting rate increases with the increase of rotational speed before 100 s. As can be seen from Fig. 8(c), rotation accelerates the overall internal temperature of PCM. Compared with the model with no rotational speed, the average internal temperature of PCM increases significantly at a low rotational speed of 0.05 rpm. At n = 0.5 rpm and 1 rpm, the average internal temperature of the PCM is very similar. In addition, the evolution of the liquid components of the two is very similar. Fig. 8(d) quantifies the total melting time corresponding to each rotational speed model and the percentage saving of melting time compared with the model without rotational speed. Compared with the no-rotation model, the melting times of 0.05 and 0.1 rpm decreased by 40.94% and 46.98%. Fig. 8(e) and (f), respectively, give the sensible heat energy absorption and total heat absorption of T-LHTESU in a melting cycle at different speeds. Because the latent heat absorption is only related to the liquid rate evolution, the changing trend is consistent with the liquid rate evolution. Also, the rotation does not change the total amount of latent heat absorption in the melting cycle, which is not described here. Except for the higher sensible heat absorption at 0.1 rpm, the sensible heat /total heat absorption decreases gradually with the increase of the rotational speed. It can be seen that the slope of the liquid phase ratio decreases before the 0.1 rpm model approaches complete melting from Fig. 8(a), which delays the completion of the melting process and absorbs more heat. Fig. 8(g) quantitatively compares the total melting time and heat absorption. Combined with Fig. 8(d), it can be found that the addition of rotation shortens the time to complete melting, but overall the sensible heat/total heat absorption is reduced. Compared with the no-rotation model, the sensible heat absorption of 0.05 and 0.1 rpm decreased by 5.52 and 2.498%, and the total heat absorption decreased by 1.48% and 0.67%, respectively. This indicates that the addition of rotational speed can greatly shorten the time required for complete melting of T-LHTESU, but reduce the absorption of sensible heat energy to a certain extent. The total absorption of T-LHTESU during the whole melting cycle has a certain decrease, but it is not obvious. Then, the melting parameters corresponding to higher rotational speeds of 0.5 and 1 rpm were observed. Compared with the no-rotation model, the melting time of the two models decreased by 68 and 69.35%, the sensible heat absorption decreased by 7.55 and 8.07%, and the total heat absorption decreased by 2.03% and 2.17%, respectively. There is little difference between the models under the effect of the two rotational speeds, which shows that increasing the rotational speed arbitrarily does not necessarily effectively promote the heat transfer rate of T-LHTESU, and increasing the speed will lead to a decrease in heat absorption. Therefore, Fig. 8(h) shows the comparison of specific changes and average heat transfer rates at different rotational speeds. The average heat transfer rate of 0.05 rpm increased by 40.04% compared to the no-rotation model, while the average heat transfer rate of 0.5 and 1 rpm is only 4.06% different. Therefore, the increase in heat transfer rate brought by low rotational speed is significant, and the increase of heat transfer rate decreases gradually with the increase of rotational speed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dynamic temperature and flow velocity study
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the specific influence of rotation on the internal heat transfer mechanism of T-LHTESU, this part conducts a specific study on the changes in internal temperature and flow velocity during the melting process of the no-rotation model and 0.1 rpm speed model. For the dynamic study, the temperature/flow rate points shown in Fig. 2(b) are selected. And the obtained results are shown in Figs. 9 and 10 , respectively.

Firstly, observe the temperature response inside the no-rotation model from Fig. 9(a),(c), and (e). It can be found that Point 2 and Point 8 in this region maintain low-temperature for the longest time, so the refraction zone in T-LHTESU is concentrated at this position. When t > 300 s, it is found that the temperature of these two points changes abruptly, and finally reaches the temperature required for PCM melting at this position. From Fig. 9(a) and (b), it is found that the temperature response of Point 1 fluctuates to a certain extent. During 200 ∼ 300 s, the flow velocity of Point 1 changes dramatically and reaches the highest in Points 1–3. This is caused by the interaction between heat conduction and natural convection at Point 1, and similar phenomena can also be found at Point 4 and Point 7. Through the comparison of flow velocity, it can be found that Points 1, 4, and 8 correspond to the highest rate among the three points and Point 8 has the highest velocity among all points. This is because the natural convection in the refraction zone is very small in the heating process, and heat conduction brings the maximum dynamic flow velocity response to it, which also explains the temperature mutation at Point 8.

Now the internal response of the model with a 0.1 rpm rotation speed is compared and analyzed. It can be found that rotation completely changes the internal temperature response of T-LHTESU. Taking Points 1–3 as examples, the region at Point 2 is originally difficult to melt, but at this time it reaches the phase transition temperature of PCM earlier. In addition, Point 1 becomes a relatively hard-to-melt region. This is because Point 1 is far away from the fin and requires a certain amount of time to conduct heat transfer under the action of a low speed of 0.1 rpm, which keeps this Point at a constant low-temperature state for a long time. However, Point 3 shows a temperature fallback phenomenon, which is due to the position change of the internal low-temperature solid PCM caused by rotation. The same phenomenon can also be found in Point 6. According to the flow velocity response in Fig. 10(b), it can be seen that the addition of rotation intensifies the internal flow velocity changes of Points 2 and 3 and weakens the overall flow velocity of Point 1, but the maximum flow velocity of Point 1 is still the largest. This indicates that the heat transfer effect of Points 2 and 3 is strengthened by rotation. Also, it is worth noting that the initial instantaneous flow velocity is basically consistent with the tangential velocity, and then decreases rapidly and approaches 0. This is because in the initial stage of melting, the solid PCM is rotated so that the flow velocity generated by natural convection and the flow velocity generated by rotation cancel out each other. Until the PCM at this position gradually melts, the flow velocity at this location tends to be stable, which is approximately equal to the initial instantaneous flow velocity, and the ratio of the flow velocity is approximately the ratio of the distances from each point to the center of the circle. This is because the PCM becomes completely liquid at this time, and the velocity at each point is the rotation speed to overcome the viscous force. After a comparison of the maximum flow velocity, it can be found that the maximum flow velocity of Point 4 in the 0.1 rpm model is higher than those of the other Points. This is because Point 4 is closest to the top fin, resulting in temperature fluctuation and strong flow velocity response. Compared with the no-rotation model, Points 7–9 reduced the maximum velocity, but the overall velocity fluctuation is more intense. This indicates that the rotation improves the heat transfer at the position of Points 7–9, and the deposition phenomenon caused by the original thermal buoyancy no longer exists in the model with rotation.

The Response surface method (RSM) [49,50] is used as a statistical and mathematical method to reduce experimental and computational costs and is commonly used in engineering to determine the system response, especially when the system response depends on the influence of different variables. The method first determines the experimental design according to the variables, then establishes the empirical model. The first and second order polynomial equations are usually used to fit the test results and perform optimization. The second-order model is usually as follows:
(25)

where, the design factor and tuning parameters are represented by X and a, respectively, Y is the system response, and n is the number of factors. The optimization design based on RSM is determined by the expected value, which ranges from 0 to 1, and the system is optimal when the expected value is 1.

The design variables selected in this study and their ranges are shown in Table 2 . Fin length ∈ [3 mm, 7 mm], fin width ∈ [0.5 mm, 1 mm], and fin rotation angle ∈ [-15°, 15°] are selected as design factors. The system response is set as the complete melting time of T-LHTESU, and the expected value is the minimum melting time of T-LHTESU. Table 3 shows the quadratic model variance analysis of RSM. The F-value of the model is 235.72 and the P-value < 0.0001 indicate that the model is resultful. With the F-value equal to 2.54 and the P-value equal to 0.0559 of the model's misfitting items are not significant, which are also the required results. R 2 = 0.9967 indicates that more than 99% of the models are controlled by parameters, while the difference between Adjusted R 2 and Predicted R 2 is less than 0.2 indicating a reasonable consistency. In addition, the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) is measured by Adeq Precision, and > 4 is ideal. Now SNR = 43.1522, shows that the model is feasible for response surface analysis.

The RSM research results are shown in Fig. 11 . Firstly, a comparison between the predicted value and the actual value is shown in Fig. 11(a). The predicted value and the actual value are basically in the same line, which indicates that the subsequent prediction analysis of the system response is effective. The influences of various design factors on the complete melting time of T-LHTESU are shown in Fig. 11(b),(c), and (d), respectively. The left figures are the three-dimensional response, and the figures on the right represent its projection and contour line in the X plane, to observe the specific influence of each factor more easily on the melting time. It can be seen that the complete melting time of T-LHTESU increases with the increase of factor 1 (fin length, L) and factor 2 (fin width, w), while the increase of factor 3 (fin rotation angle, θ) makes the melting time to decrease first and then increase. The fluid-structure coupling equation of the above parameters with respect to melting time is as follows:
(26)

RSM cannot only study the effect of multiple parameters on the response (Melting time) but also predict the minimum melting time. The results show that when L = 7 mm, w = 1 mm, θ = −2.66°, the melting time is minimum, and the expected value is 1. This Model is defined as Model-R. According to the calculation, the corresponding complete melting time of Model-R at 0.1 rpm is 208 s, which is 12.24% lower than that of the original Model at 0.1 rpm. This is optimized under the condition of increasing the fin width. Compared with the original model, the PCM volume of the optimized model is reduced by 1.5%, which is acceptable. It can be seen from the curvature and Eq. (24) of the three-dimensional response that among the three variables, the fin length has the greatest effect on the target response (T-LHTESU complete melting time). In the future, in-depth research on the specific effect of fin length on melting time expands the range of factor variables.

Based on Section 4.3, this part conducts research on the optimal structure Model-R. Firstly, the liquid phase distribution, temperature distribution, and flow velocity distribution of Model-R under the action of 0.1 rpm are investigated, and then the influence of physical factors (wall temperature and fin/tube wall material) is studied.

As shown in Fig. 12 , Model-R at 0.1 rpm rotation speed only changes the fin width and fin rotation angle based on the original Model, so its liquid phase distribution and temperature distribution at every moment are not significantly different from those of the original Model. As a whole, the melting area of the PCM region after fin rotation increases continuously due to the effect of rotation, and the low-temperature area is mainly concentrated in the front end of fin rotation. When t = 180 s, only a small area of solid PCM in the upper left part of Model-R remains unmelted. Compared with the initial model at 0.1 rpm rotation speed in Fig. 6, the area of solid PCM is reduced. From the flow velocity distribution in Fig. 12, the maximum flow velocity of Model-R at t = 120 s increases by 37.5% compared with the original Model, and the maximum flow velocity at other times is almost the same. This proves that in the later melting stage, the internal convective intensity of Model-R is higher than that of the original Model, thus reducing the melting time by 12.24%.

The influence of wall temperature on the melting process based on Model-R at 0.1 rpm rotation speed is studied in this section. Among them, wall temperature includes tube wall and fin root temperature. Besides the initial Tw = 329.15 K, the wall temperatures of Tw = 320.15, 323.15, 326.15, and 332.15 K are selected, and the initial PCM temperature is still 290.15 K.

Fig. 13 shows the influence of wall temperature on liquid phase rate evolution, melting rate, heat absorption rate, and absorbed heat. In Fig. 13(a), it can be found that the change in wall temperature changes the temperature difference between the wall and PCM, and higher wall temperature makes the fin temperature rise more easily in a short time. The increase in temperature difference improves the heat transfer efficiency. Therefore, the higher Tw is, the greater the temperature difference between the wall surface and PCM is, and the shorter the melting time is. Fig. 13(b) shows a comparison of melting rates. Except at the end of melting, the higher Tw leads to a higher overall melting rate. When Tw = 329.15 and 332.15 K, the melting rate increases temporarily at t = 100 s, which is not available at low wall temperature. From Fig. 13(c) and (d), it is found that the higher Tw is, the higher the growth rate of sensible heat storage energy is, and the higher the sensible heat storage energy is in the melting cycle. A comparison of specific energy storage and melting time is shown in Fig. 13(f). When Tw increases from 320.15 to 332.15 K, the sensible heat storage energy increases by 29.44%, the total energy increases by 6.49%, and the complete melting time of Model-R decreases by 44.06%. Fig. 13(e) shows the comparison of heat absorption rates corresponding to different wall temperatures. When the Tw increases from 320.15 to 332.15 K, the average heat absorption rate increases by 90.37%. It is proved that the increase of the temperature difference between wall temperature and PCM is beneficial to the improvement of heat absorption rate, and it decreases the melting time and increases the energy storage during the melting cycle.

The effect of the fin/tube wall material of Model-R at 0.1 rpm rotation speed on the melting properties is studied in this section. Except for the initial material copper, aluminum, nickel, steel, and titanium are selected as fin/tube wall materials for the study. The thermal physical properties of copper are shown in Table 1, and the related properties of other materials are shown in Table 4 .

As shown in Fig. 14 , the effect of fin/tube wall material on liquid phase evolution, melting rate, heat absorption rate, and absorbed heat. As evident from Fig. 14(a), among the five materials with the highest thermal conductivity, copper has the fastest liquid phase evolution, but there is a slight difference between aluminum and copper. Titanium with the lowest thermal conductivity has the slowest evolution of the liquid phase. According to the comparison of melting speed in Fig. 14(b), the melting rates of copper and aluminum are the fastest, and there is a rebound phenomenon at about t = 125 s. Fig. 14(c) and (d) show the changes in sensible heat storage and total energy storage over time. Combined with Fig. 14(f) showing energy storage and melting time, it can be found that the sensible heat storage and total energy storage of steel are higher than for the other four materials in melting time. The total energy storage of steel increased by 0.78% compared with copper, but the melting time increased by 35.58%. Among them, titanium has the lowest thermal conductivity, and the melting time is 39.9% higher than copper, while the total energy storage is only 0.38% higher. Fig. 14(e) shows the comparison of heat absorption rates of different fin/tube wall materials. The thermal conductivity of titanium is 98.08% lower than for copper, but the average heat absorption is only 28.25% lower than that of copper. This is because the addition of the rotation mechanism makes the thermal conductivity less pronounced than in static melting, but it is more effective to use copper or aluminum with high thermal conductivity and low cost as the fin/tube wall material.

In this paper, the effect of the rotation mechanism on the melt properties of T-LHTESU is investigated. The effects of different rotating speeds on liquid phase distribution, temperature distribution, energy storage and energy storage efficiency are studied, and the improvement of heat transfer means by the rotating speed mechanism is illustrated through the dynamic temperature and flow rate responses. Response surface method is used to optimize the design of the unit structure, and the specific influence of relevant physical factors on the melt properties is studied. The main conclusions are as follows:
(1)
The addition of a rotation mechanism significantly improves the melting properties of the T-LHTESU. Compared with the stationary model, the melting time of the unit at the rotation speeds of 0.1 and 1 rpm is reduced by 46.98 and 69.35%, the total stored heat energy is reduced by 0.67 and 2.17%, and the heat storage efficiency is increased by 87.34% and 219.19%, respectively. The decrease of the total stored heat energy is due to the longer melting time in the stationary model or the low rotational speed model and the larger area of the internal high temperature zone.
(2)
The addition of rotation can improve the heat deposition phenomenon caused by natural convection in the static model, increase the overall internal convection intensity, and improve the heat transfer efficiency of the melting process. However, the rotation mechanism has a negative impact on the total amount of heat storage, and blindly increasing the rotation speed has a limited effect on the increase in heat absorption efficiency.
(3)
According to the response surface method, the fin length has the most significant influence on the melting time compared with the fin width and the fin angle. The optimized model has better heat transfer performance in the later stage of melting, and the melting time is further shortened by 12.24%.
(4)
Increasing the temperature difference between wall surface and PCM is beneficial to improve the heat absorption rate of melt, shorten the melt time, and improve the energy storage capacity during the melt cycle. However, the thermal conductivity of the fin/tube wall material has little influence on the melting performance due to the addition of rotation.
(5)
As for the follow-up research on the rotational mechanism in the T-LHTES, it is suggested to extend to higher speed, carry out system-related research, apply to a wider range of PCMs, and conduct economic analysis on the power consumption of the torque required by the application speed, which is conducive to promoting the large-scale application of the new mechanism in the industry in the future.
